section.title: Setting Up the Tabernacle
section.sectionRationale: This final chapter of Exodus brings the whole book to its intended goal: Moses sets up the tabernacle exactly as commanded, and the cloud covers the Tent of Meeting as the glory of the LORD fills the tabernacle, so powerfully that even Moses cannot enter, closing the book with God's presence dwelling visibly among His redeemed people.
section.thesis: Moses sets up and consecrates the tabernacle precisely as the LORD commanded, and the glory of the LORD fills the finished dwelling so completely that not even Moses can enter, with the cloud and fire then leading Israel onward through all their journeys.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the command to set up the tabernacle on the first day of the new year; Moses' exact obedience in erecting and consecrating every piece; and the glory of the LORD filling the tabernacle, with the cloud guiding Israel's ongoing journey.

units.label: Set up the tabernacle
units.note: on the first day of the first month

media.alt: A tent structure being raised at dawn
media.caption: The tabernacle is set up on the first day of the new year, exactly as commanded.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“On the first day of the first month you are to set up the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting.
Put the ark of the Testimony in it and screen off the ark with the veil.
Then bring in the table and set out its arrangement; bring in the lampstand as well, and set up its lamps.
Place the gold altar of incense in front of the ark of the Testimony, and hang the curtain at the entrance to the tabernacle.
Place the altar of burnt offering in front of the entrance to the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting.
And place the basin between the Tent of Meeting and the altar, and put water in it.
Set up the surrounding courtyard and hang the curtain for the entrance to the courtyard.
Take the anointing oil and anoint the tabernacle and everything in it; consecrate it along with all its furnishings, and it shall be holy.
Anoint the altar of burnt offering and all its utensils; consecrate the altar, and it shall be most holy.
Anoint the basin and its stand and consecrate them.
Then bring Aaron and his sons to the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and wash them with water.
And you are to clothe Aaron with the holy garments, anoint him, and consecrate him, so that he may serve Me as a priest.
Bring his sons forward and clothe them with tunics.
Anoint them just as you anointed their father, so that they may also serve Me as priests. Their anointing will qualify them for a permanent priesthood throughout their generations.”

units.label: Moses did everything just as commanded
units.note: he laid its bases, positioned its frames

verses.text: Moses did everything just as the LORD had commanded him.
So the tabernacle was set up on the first day of the first month of the second year.
When Moses set up the tabernacle, he laid its bases, positioned its frames, inserted its crossbars, and set up its posts.
Then he spread the tent over the tabernacle and put the covering over the tent, just as the LORD had commanded him.
Moses took the Testimony and placed it in the ark, attaching the poles to the ark; and he set the mercy seat atop the ark.
Then he brought the ark into the tabernacle, put up the veil for the screen, and shielded off the ark of the Testimony, just as the LORD had commanded him.
Moses placed the table in the Tent of Meeting on the north side of the tabernacle, outside the veil.
He arranged the bread on it before the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded him.
He also placed the lampstand in the Tent of Meeting opposite the table on the south side of the tabernacle
and set up the lamps before the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded him.
Moses placed the gold altar in the Tent of Meeting, in front of the veil,
and he burned fragrant incense on it, just as the LORD had commanded him.
Then he put up the curtain at the entrance to the tabernacle.
He placed the altar of burnt offering near the entrance to the tabernacle, the Tent of Meeting, and offered on it the burnt offering and the grain offering, just as the LORD had commanded him.
He placed the basin between the Tent of Meeting and the altar and put water in it for washing;
and from it Moses, Aaron, and his sons washed their hands and feet.
They washed whenever they entered the Tent of Meeting or approached the altar, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
And Moses set up the courtyard around the tabernacle and the altar, and he hung the curtain for the entrance to the courtyard. So Moses finished the work.

media.alt: A radiant cloud settling over a tent
media.caption: The glory of the LORD fills the tabernacle, and Moses cannot enter.

verses.text: Then the cloud covered the Tent of Meeting, and the {{glory|glory}} of the LORD {{filled|filled}} the tabernacle.
Moses was unable to enter the Tent of Meeting because the cloud had settled on it, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
Whenever the cloud was lifted from above the tabernacle, the Israelites would set out through all the stages of their journey.
If the cloud was not lifted, they would not set out until the day it was taken up.
For the cloud of the LORD was over the tabernacle by day, and fire was in the cloud by night, in the sight of all the house of Israel through all their journeys.

glory.gloss: Glory, weightiness, the visible manifestation of God's presence. Here the kavod does not merely appear near the tabernacle but fills it completely, so overwhelming that even Moses, who spoke with God face to face, cannot enter.

filled.gloss: To fill, to be full. The same verb used of the earth being filled with God's glory in Isaiah 6:3 and of the temple filled with glory in 1 Kings 8:11 describes this climactic moment: the tabernacle, built for exactly this purpose, is now completely filled with God's own presence.

basic.context: This final chapter of Exodus shows Israel finally setting up the tabernacle exactly a year after leaving Egypt, following God's instructions in careful order: the ark, table, lampstand, altars, and courtyard, each consecrated with anointing oil. When everything is complete, the cloud covers the tent and the glory of the LORD fills the tabernacle so fully that even Moses cannot enter.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the historical climax of the whole book of Exodus: what began with slaves crying out under Egyptian oppression ends with God's own glory dwelling visibly among them. The chapter's meaning is not hidden but explicit, stated back in Exodus 29:45-46: God's whole purpose was to dwell among His people, and here that purpose is finally, gloriously realized.
basic.originalLanguages: The kavod, glory, of the LORD does not simply hover near the tabernacle but is male, fills, it entirely, using the same verb later applied to the temple filled with glory in 1 Kings 8:11 and the whole earth filled with God's glory in Isaiah 6:3. This filling is so complete and overwhelming that even Moses, unique among all people in speaking with God face to face, cannot enter.
basic.typology: The glory filling the tabernacle anticipates the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled' among us, and John says 'we have seen His glory,' John 1:14. It also anticipates the final new creation, where God's glory will fill and illuminate the new Jerusalem completely, needing no other light, Revelation 21:23.
basic.christ: John deliberately echoes this chapter when he writes that the Word became flesh and dwelt, or tabernacled, among us, and we beheld His glory, John 1:14, presenting Jesus as the fulfillment of everything this filled tabernacle pictured: God's glory dwelling bodily and visibly among His people. Where Moses could not even enter the tent because of the glory's intensity, in Christ, God's glory dwells among humanity without destroying those who draw near in faith.
basic.god: God's entire purpose throughout Exodus, redemption from slavery, covenant, law, and tabernacle, converges here: He dwells among His people so completely that His glory fills their midst. This is not a distant, occasional visitation but a settled, continual presence, guiding Israel's every step through the cloud by day and fire by night.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:14 says the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld His glory. 1 Kings 8:10-11 records the same glory filling Solomon's temple at its dedication. Revelation 21:22-23 describes the new Jerusalem needing no temple or sun, since God's glory itself is its light.
basic.oneStory: Exodus began with an oppressed people crying out under slavery and ends with God's own glory filling their midst, guiding them by cloud and fire through every stage of their journey. That glory, once so overwhelming even Moses could not approach it, is fully and permanently revealed in Jesus Christ, whose glory believers now behold, and it will one day fill the whole new creation.

context.literary: This chapter closes the entire book of Exodus by fulfilling the purpose stated repeatedly throughout the tabernacle instructions, that God may dwell among His people, moving from precise, obedient construction to the overwhelming, climactic filling of the tabernacle with God's glory.
context.historical: The tabernacle is set up exactly one year after the exodus from Egypt, on the first day of the first month of the second year, marking the completion of an entire year's journey from slavery to a fully consecrated dwelling place for God among His people.
context.canonical: This scene of glory filling a completed sanctuary becomes a recurring pattern in Scripture, echoed at the dedication of Solomon's temple and pointing forward to both the incarnation of Christ and the final, complete filling of the new creation with God's glory in Revelation.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the completion of the tabernacle and the fulfillment of God's stated purpose to dwell among His people, showing that His glory truly and overwhelmingly filled what they had faithfully built according to His pattern.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical narrative describing the completion and consecration of Israel's specific tabernacle; it does not prescribe a ritual for consecrating buildings today. Its abiding truth is God's desire and ability to dwell among His people, fulfilled completely in Christ and anticipated fully in the new creation.

debates.question: Why could Moses, who spoke with God face to face in the tent of meeting in chapter 33, not enter the tabernacle once the glory filled it here?

views.label: The sheer intensity of the glory itself
views.case: Many interpreters see this simply as the glory's overwhelming physical and spiritual intensity at this particular moment of dedication, distinct from the ongoing, regulated access Moses and the priests would have afterward through the prescribed rituals and the veil.

views.label: A deliberate mark of the tabernacle's completion and consecration
views.case: Others see this moment as intentionally marking the definitive, formal consecration of the space, similar to the priests being unable to minister when glory filled Solomon's temple in 1 Kings 8:11, a sign confirming God's acceptance of the completed work before regular priestly access resumed.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same central point: this was an extraordinary, climactic moment of divine confirmation and presence, distinct from the tabernacle's ongoing, regulated daily use that would follow.

study.originalLanguages: The kavod, glory, of the LORD does not merely appear but is described as male, filling, the tabernacle entirely, the same word used of the temple filled with glory in 1 Kings 8:11 and the whole earth filled with God's glory in Isaiah 6:3. This filling was so complete that even Moses, unique in speaking with God face to face, Exodus 33:11, could not enter the tent at this moment.

typology.type: The glory of the LORD filling the tabernacle
typology.fulfillment: The Word becoming flesh and tabernacling among us, His glory beheld by those who trust in Him, John 1:14.

typology.type: The cloud by day and fire by night guiding Israel's every journey
typology.fulfillment: God's continual, guiding presence with His people, ultimately through the indwelling Holy Spirit, John 14:16-17.

study.christ: John's declaration that the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and 'we have seen His glory, the glory of the one and only Son,' John 1:14, deliberately echoes this very scene, presenting Jesus as the fulfillment of everything the filled tabernacle pictured. Where the glory here was so intense that even Moses could not enter, in Christ, God's glory has drawn near to humanity in a way that welcomes rather than excludes all who come to Him in faith.
study.god: God's entire redemptive purpose throughout Exodus, delivering Israel from slavery, giving His law, providing atonement, culminates in this moment: His own glory fills the dwelling built for exactly this purpose, so overwhelmingly that not even Moses can enter. Yet this same God then leads His people continually, by cloud and fire, through every stage of their ongoing journey, showing His presence is not a single dramatic moment but a settled, guiding companionship.

crossReferences.note: The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld His glory.
The glory of the LORD similarly fills Solomon's temple at its dedication.
The new Jerusalem needs no temple or sun, for the glory of God is its light.

study.oneStory: Exodus begins with a people groaning under slavery and ends with the glory of the LORD filling the tabernacle they built for Him, so completely that even Moses cannot enter, and that same presence then guides them faithfully through every stage of their journey. This glory finds its fullest revelation in Jesus Christ, in whom God's glory tabernacles among humanity and draws near rather than excludes, and it will one day fill the entire new creation forever.

quiz.q: On what specific day was the tabernacle set up, according to verse 17?

options.text: The first day of the first month of the second year.
options.note: Verse 17 states this exact date, one year after the exodus from Egypt.

options.text: The seventh day of the seventh month.
options.note: The text specifically dates the setup to the first month of the second year, not the seventh month.

options.text: The tenth day of the tenth month.
options.note: No such date is given; verse 17 specifies the first day of the first month.

options.text: No specific date is given.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly states the exact date of the tabernacle's setup.

quiz.q: What repeated phrase describes how Moses carried out the LORD's instructions in setting up the tabernacle?

options.text: Just as the LORD had commanded him.
options.note: This exact refrain repeats throughout verses 19 to 32.

options.text: According to Aaron's personal preference.
options.note: The repeated refrain credits obedience to the LORD's command, not Aaron's preference.

options.text: With significant modifications for practicality.
options.note: The text emphasizes exact conformity to command, not modification for convenience.

options.text: No such refrain appears in this chapter.
options.note: The phrase just as the LORD had commanded him is explicitly repeated multiple times.

quiz.q: What happened when Moses finished setting up the tabernacle, according to verse 34?

options.text: The cloud covered the Tent of Meeting, and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
options.note: Verse 34 records this exact, climactic event.

options.text: A fire consumed an offering on the altar as a sign of approval.
options.note: While fire consuming an offering occurs elsewhere in Scripture, this specific verse describes the cloud and glory filling the tabernacle.

options.text: Nothing unusual occurred; the people simply began daily worship.
options.note: Verse 34 explicitly describes the extraordinary filling of the tabernacle with God's glory.

options.text: The text does not describe any event at this point.
options.note: Verse 34 explicitly records the cloud and the glory of the LORD filling the tabernacle.

quiz.q: Why was Moses unable to enter the Tent of Meeting after it was set up, according to verse 35?

options.text: Because the cloud had settled on it and the glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle.
options.note: Verse 35 states this exact reason.

options.text: Because Aaron had locked the entrance.
options.note: No such human obstruction is mentioned; the reason given is the overwhelming presence of God's glory.

options.text: Because Moses was ceremonially unclean at the time.
options.note: The text specifies the glory's intensity as the reason, not any ceremonial uncleanness on Moses' part.

options.text: No reason is given.
options.note: Verse 35 explicitly explains that the cloud and glory prevented his entry.

quiz.q: How did the Israelites know when to set out on each stage of their wilderness journey, according to verses 36 to 38?

options.text: By watching whether the cloud lifted from above the tabernacle.
options.note: Verses 36 to 38 describe this exact guiding pattern of the cloud and fire.

options.text: By a trumpet blown every morning regardless of circumstances.
options.note: The described guidance came through the cloud lifting or remaining, not a fixed daily trumpet signal.

options.text: By counting a fixed number of days at each camp.
options.note: The text specifies the cloud's movement, not a predetermined day count, as the guide for setting out.

options.text: No guidance system is described.
options.note: Verses 36 through 38 explicitly describe the cloud's movement as Israel's guide.

questions: The tabernacle was completed exactly one year after Israel left Egypt, the culmination of a long process of redemption, law, failure, and renewal. Where in your own life is God patiently building something over a long season that you have not yet seen completed?

The glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle so completely that even Moses could not enter. How does this scene shape your sense of the weight and wonder of God's presence, compared to how casually you might sometimes approach Him?

Israel's movement through the wilderness depended entirely on watching whether the cloud lifted, not their own schedule or preference. Where in your own life do you need to wait on God's timing rather than pushing ahead on your own initiative?
The whole book of Exodus moves from slavery to God's glory dwelling visibly among His people. How does this arc, from bondage to God's presence, describe your own journey with God so far?

John says the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, and we beheld His glory, directly echoing this chapter. How does knowing that God's glory now dwells among humanity in Jesus, rather than in a tent no one could enter, change how you approach Him today?

translationNotes.note: The first day of the first month marks exactly one year since the exodus from Egypt, Exodus 12:2, showing the tabernacle's completion as the culmination of Israel's first full year as a redeemed nation.

The glory of the LORD filled the tabernacle uses the same Hebrew verb, male, later used of the temple filled with glory in 1 Kings 8:11 and the whole earth filled with God's glory in Isaiah 6:3, linking this moment to a recurring biblical pattern of God's presence filling sacred and, ultimately, cosmic space.

Through all their journeys closes the book of Exodus on a forward-looking note, since the cloud and fire would continue guiding Israel well beyond this moment, a pattern continued and explained further in the book of Numbers.
